figures.gloss: Born Okada Yoshikazu (1901–1974), a former Imperial Army officer and member of Sekai Kyūsei Kyō who reported that on 27 February 1959 the creator God commanded him: 'The time of heaven has come. Rise. Thy name shall be Kōtama (Jewel of Light). Raise thy hand.' He founded the Mahikari movement to transmit divine light through the human palm, teaching that a fiery cleansing of civilization was near.
figures.facet: A founder whose revelation arrived as a job transfer within an existing lineage — Okada carried Sekai Kyūsei Kyō's light-channeling template into a new dispensation, illustrating how Japan's postwar new religions bud from one another rather than from nothing.
figures.provenance: Goseigen (compiled revelations of Okada Kōtama); Winston Davis, Dōjō: Magic and Exorcism in Modern Japan (1980); Brian J. McVeigh, Spirits, Selves, and Subjectivity in a Japanese New Religion (1997).

figures.gloss: The central practice: a practitioner raises an open palm toward another person's forehead and body, radiating divine light held to purify spiritual toxins and pacify attaching ancestral and animal spirits, whose disturbances are diagnosed as the root of illness and misfortune. Any initiate who has received the amulet may give light — laity included, from the first day.
figures.facet: Thaumaturgy fully democratized: where most traditions reserve channeling for specialists, Mahikari hands the healing act to every member at initiation, making the rite itself the movement's recruitment engine.
figures.provenance: Winston Davis, Dōjō: Magic and Exorcism in Modern Japan (1980 — participant-observer study of okiyome practice); McVeigh (1997).

figures.gloss: The consecrated locket received at the three-day initiation course, worn on the body and treated as the conduit through which Su God's light flows to the bearer's palm. It is hedged with strict handling taboos — it must never touch the ground, get wet, or be casually handled — and losing it severs the ability to give light until it is restored.
figures.facet: The sacred rendered portable and conditional: authority to channel divine power resides not in the person's attainment but in a small object's intact consecration, concentrating the whole tradition's discipline into the care of a locket.

figures.gloss: The origin creator God of the universe, whose light all authentic religions are taught to have refracted in partial forms. Mahikari holds that the era of divided religions is closing and Su God is now moving humanity toward a unified, light-centered civilization — with a purifying 'baptism by fire' preceding it.
figures.facet: Monotheism framed as convergence rather than exclusion: rival faiths are not false but downstream — a claim of one root beneath all religions inherited recognizably from the Oomoto lineage's bankyō dōkon.

figures.gloss: The canonical scripture: revelations Okada Kōtama reported receiving from Su God between 1959 and his death in 1974, compiled as direct divine speech in an archaic, oracular Japanese. It grounds the movement's cosmology of spirit disturbance, purification, and the coming change of civilizations.
figures.facet: Scripture as verbatim dictation within living memory — the text's authority rests on the founder's mediumship being recent, datable, and organizationally continuous, not on antiquity.
figures.provenance: Goseigen (Sekai Mahikari Bunmei Kyōdan 1970; Sukyo Mahikari editions thereafter); Davis (1980).

figures.gloss: The monumental World Main Shrine completed in November 1984 at Takayama in the Japan Alps under Okada Keishu, the founder's successor — built as the seat of Su God on earth and the ritual center toward which the worldwide movement orients, with a golden roof and a great hall for thousands.
figures.facet: An axis mundi constructed on schedule and by contract in the 1980s — the world-center pattern executed as a modern building project, its sanctity dated to a ribbon-cutting rather than to myth.
figures.provenance: Sukyo Mahikari organizational history (Suza dedication, 1984); McVeigh (1997).

figures.gloss: The founder's adopted daughter (1929-2016) and first disciple. After Okada Kōtama's 1974 death, she contested his named successor Sekiguchi Sakae in an eight-year court case, then in 1978 broke away to found Sukyo Mahikari as a separate organization, serving as its second Oshienushi (spiritual leader) until her own death.
figures.facet: The tradition's living infrastructure is as much her legacy as her father's revelation: she built the Suza World Main Shrine (dedicated 1984) and carried the movement's practice to South America, Europe, and Oceania, turning a contested inheritance into a global institution.
figures.provenance: Brian J. McVeigh, Spirits, Selves, and Subjectivity in a Japanese New Religion (1997); Sukyo Mahikari's own published organizational history.

figures.gloss: The central purification prayer, recited aloud in archaic Japanese, addressed to Su God at the opening of okiyome — asking for protection, the purification of spirits contrary to divine law, and the revival of the practitioner's true nature as a child of God.
figures.facet: The rite's necessary verbal component: the raised palm does not act alone; giving light is bound to, and activated by, this specific recited formula, making the words as load-bearing as the gesture.
figures.provenance: Winston Davis, Dōjō: Magic and Exorcism in Modern Japan (1980); Sukyo Mahikari liturgical texts derived from the Goseigen.

figures.gloss: The 'innermost attitude of mind' members are taught to monitor and align with Su God's will — a disposition held to determine whether one's circumstances purify (improve) or worsen, independent of outward behavior.
figures.facet: Locates moral agency almost entirely in interior disposition rather than visible action: the same outward deed can purify or defile depending on the sonen behind it, an ethics of hidden intention rather than deed.
figures.provenance: Brian J. McVeigh, Spirits, Selves, and Subjectivity in a Japanese New Religion (1997) — the monograph's organizing concept.

edges.note: Documented lineage with one intermediate step: Okada Kōtama was a Sekai Kyūsei Kyō member, whose founder Okada Mokichi developed jōrei light-channeling after his years as an Oomoto leader — okiyome descends from the Oomoto spirit-pacification milieu through that chain.

The one-root-of-all-religions claim: Mahikari's teaching that all faiths refract Su God's single light recognizably continues the bankyō dōkon universalism Onisaburō preached — a lineage-plausible echo, tiered as analogy since direct doctrinal transmission is not documented.

Healing power transmitted through the raised hand of the ordinary believer — okiyome and Pentecostal divine healing both democratize the channeling of sacred power to the laity, arising independently in the same century.
Intra-tradition: the rite and its enabling object — light can be given only while the consecrated pendant is worn and intact; the practice runs through the locket.
Intra-tradition: the recited prayer that opens every session of the rite it belongs to.
Intra-tradition: the practitioner's rightly-aligned sonen is taught as the condition determining whether light received through okiyome purifies or fails to.
Intra-tradition: adopted daughter, first disciple, and successor to the founder, splitting from his named heir to found Sukyo Mahikari in his stead.
Intra-tradition: she commissioned and dedicated the World Main Shrine (1984) as the movement's global ritual center.

The one-root-of-all-religions claim now anchored to its own dedicated figure: Mahikari's teaching that all faiths refract Su God's single light recognizably continues the universalism Onisaburō preached as bankyō dōkon — lineage-plausible echo, tiered as analogy since direct doctrinal transmission is not documented.

dossier.essence: A Japanese new religion founded by Okada Kōtama (1901-1974), a former Imperial Army officer and Sekai Kyūsei Kyō member who reported that on 27 February 1959 the creator God commanded him to rise, take the name Kōtama ('Jewel of Light'), and raise his hand to transmit divine light. After his death the movement split in a succession dispute; Sukyo Mahikari, incorporated in 1978 under his successor Okada Keishu, is the larger branch, practicing at dōjō centers across Japan and internationally from Europe and Africa to the Americas and Australia.
dossier.cosmos: Su God (Mioya Motosu Mahikari Ōmikami) is the origin creator whose single light all authentic religions partially refract; the era of divided faiths is closing, and a purifying 'baptism by fire' precedes a unified, light-centered holy civilization. Illness and misfortune are diagnosed as spiritual toxins and the disturbances of attaching spirits — above all aggrieved ancestors and animal spirits — so the unseen dead remain active parties in the living world, to be pacified and purified rather than merely mourned.
dossier.practice: The core rite is okiyome, the giving of light: any initiate who has completed the three-day kenshū course and received the consecrated omitama pendant may radiate purifying light from the raised palm toward another person's forehead and body — laity included from the first day, which makes the rite itself the movement's recruitment engine. The omitama is hedged with strict handling taboos, and members practice at local dōjō, tend ancestor altars, and orient toward Suza, the World Main Shrine completed at Takayama in 1984.
dossier.canon: The Goseigen ('Holy Words') — revelations Okada Kōtama reported receiving from Su God between 1959 and 1974, compiled as direct divine speech in archaic, oracular Japanese.
dossier.reflects: Thaumaturgy democratized — healing power placed in every believer's raised hand, with sacred authority residing in a portable consecrated object rather than in personal attainment.
sources: Winston Davis, 'Dōjō: Magic and Exorcism in Modern Japan' (1980)
Brian J. McVeigh, 'Spirits, Selves, and Subjectivity in a Japanese New Religion: The Cultural Psychology of Belief in Sūkyō Mahikari' (1997)
Catherine Cornille, 'The Phoenix Flies West: The Dynamics of the Inculturation of Mahikari in Western Europe' (1991)

links.cite: Winston Davis, 'Dōjō: Magic and Exorcism in Modern Japan' (1980)
Catherine Cornille, 'The Phoenix Flies West: The Dynamics of the Inculturation of Mahikari in Western Europe' (1991)
